era_context: The early 8th century CE continues with the Umayyad Caliphate at its territorial zenith, the Tang Dynasty flourishing under Emperor Xuanzong, and the Frankish kingdom consolidating power under Charles Martel. The Byzantine Empire is on the eve of the Iconoclastic Controversy, which will dominate religious and political life for over a century. This is a period of relative stability in major civilizations, though with underlying tensions that will soon erupt.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe and Scandinavia (sparse written records)
South Asia (limited written sources in Western archives)

events.title: Emperor Leo III's Iconoclastic policies begin — religious upheaval in Byzantine Empire
events.description: Emperor Leo III of the Byzantine Empire began implementing policies against the veneration of religious images (icons), initiating the Iconoclastic period (c. 726–843 CE). The edict was prompted by theological concerns about idolatry, military setbacks (including the loss of territories to the Umayyads and Bulgars) that were attributed to divine displeasure, and possibly administrative centralization efforts. The policy triggered intense religious and political conflict, with iconoclasts (image-breakers) opposing iconodules (image-venerators). This religious schism had profound effects on Byzantine theology, art, and relations with the Western Church and the Papacy. The exact date of the initial edict is debated (726–730 CE range), but 726 is often cited as the beginning of the Iconoclastic period.

sources.name: Theophanes the Confessor, Chronographia (written early 9th century)
Second Council of Nicaea (787 CE) — later condemnation of iconoclasm

events.certainty_note: The Iconoclastic Controversy is well-attested in later sources, but the exact date of the initial edict is debated (726–730 CE range). No contemporary imperial edict survives; the policy is reconstructed from later theological and historical accounts. Theophanes the Confessor, writing in the early 9th century, is the primary source for dating the beginning of Iconoclasm to 726.
cross_references: Related to Leo III's military reforms and theological concerns
Led to conflict with Pope Gregory II and the Western Church

events.title: Tang Dynasty cultural and administrative flourishing under Emperor Xuanzong
events.description: Emperor Xuanzong of Tang (r. 712–756) was in the midst of his reign, which is regarded as the apex of Tang power and cultural achievement. By 726, the Tang had consolidated control over much of East Asia, expanded the civil service examination system, and fostered unprecedented cultural and economic development. The period saw flourishing of poetry, painting, and cosmopolitan urban life, particularly in the capital Chang'an. The reign of Xuanzong is often called the 'Kaiyuan era' (713–741), named after the era name he adopted, which is remembered as a golden age of Chinese civilization.

sources.name: Jiu Tangshu (Old History of Tang), compiled 10th century
Xin Tangshu (New History of Tang), compiled 11th century

events.certainty_note: The reign of Xuanzong and the general trajectory of Tang power and cultural development in the early 8th century are well-attested in Chinese historical records and archaeological evidence.
cross_references: Part of broader Tang cultural flourishing (7th–8th centuries)
Precedes the An Lushan Rebellion (755–763), which destabilized the dynasty

events.title: Umayyad Caliphate under Hisham I — territorial consolidation and administrative reforms
events.region: Umayyad Caliphate (Middle East, North Africa, Iberian Peninsula, Central Asia)
events.description: Caliph Hisham I (r. 724–743) was consolidating Umayyad power and implementing administrative reforms. The Caliphate was at its territorial maximum, controlling vast territories from Spain to Central Asia. Hisham I was known for his administrative reorganization, including the expansion of the bureaucracy and military reforms. However, the Caliphate was also beginning to experience internal tensions, including religious disputes and the rise of provincial governors who would eventually challenge central authority. The reign of Hisham I is often seen as the last period of strong centralized Umayyad power before the dynasty's decline.
key_figures: Umayyad provincial governors and military commanders

sources.name: Al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: The reign of Hisham I and the general trajectory of Umayyad power in the early 8th century are well-attested in Islamic historical sources.
cross_references: Precedes the Umayyad Caliphate's decline and fall (mid-8th century)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the beginning of the Iconoclastic Controversy to 726 CE is debated among scholars. Some sources place the initial edict in 726, while others suggest 730 or even later. Theophanes the Confessor, the primary source for dating Iconoclasm to 726, was writing over 150 years after the events he describes, and his chronology is not always reliable. Modern scholarship has questioned whether a formal edict was issued at all, or whether the policy developed gradually over several years. The religious and political motivations for Iconoclasm are also debated; some scholars emphasize theological concerns about idolatry, while others stress military setbacks and administrative centralization.
historiographic_note: The year 726 CE is poorly documented in surviving sources. Most historical knowledge for this period comes from later compilations (9th–12th centuries) that synthesize earlier accounts, many of which are now lost. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean world (Byzantine Empire, Papacy, Umayyad Caliphate) and East Asia (Tang Dynasty). Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, Central Asia, and South Asia are essentially absent from the written record for this specific year. Archaeological evidence is sparse and difficult to date to a single year. The Iconoclastic Controversy is particularly poorly documented in contemporary sources; most of what we know comes from later theological and historical accounts written by both iconoclasts and iconodules, each with their own biases.

graph_edges.note: Leo III's Iconoclastic policies prompted Pope Gregory II's opposition
Iconoclastic edict initiated a controversy resolved (temporarily) by the Second Council of Nicaea in 787
Tang consolidation under Xuanzong created conditions for the An Lushan Rebellion
Umayyad consolidation under Hisham I preceded the dynasty's decline and fall in 750
